# Transcrição: 02-Homework for Life

- **Fonte:** 02-Homework for Life
- **Duração:** 31:28
- **Idioma:** en

## [00:00 - 03:00]

The first and best way for you to find stories in your everyday life is something that I call homework for life. This is going to be the most important thing that I teach you throughout this entire course. In fact, if you stop listening to the course after this particular video and you move on with your life, you will have already done a great job if you begin doing homework for life. It is transformational in the way that I view my life today and it's transformational in the way thousands, tens of thousands of people around the world view their lives today. I'm about to give you a homework assignment that you will do for the rest of your life. And it is no exaggeration to say that every single day of my life, I receive an email, a tweet, a Facebook message from someone around the world who has begun to do homework for life and is emailing me or writing to me to report to me that it's changed their lives. So I don't want you to think as I begin talking about this process that I am special in some way, that this is singularly effective just for me. It is something that everyone who does finds enormous meaning and I want that for you too. So homework for life. When I started telling stories years ago, I started to get worried that I would run out of stories to tell. I didn't want to be one of those storytellers who sort of rolled out the same old chestnut every night. Those storytellers exist. I go to New York City, I start, someone starts to tell a story and I go, I've heard this one 14 times before. I don't want to be that person. I kind of want to be the person that every single time I take a stage, I'm telling a new story. Every time I go to dinner with friends, I have something new to say. Every time I'm meeting a client, I have a new idea, a new thought from my life that I can now contribute to the meeting. I always want to have new content. And so here's what I did. I gave myself this homework assignment first. I'm an elementary school teacher. I'm inclined to give homework. It's my natural instinct. I gave this to myself first. Here's what I decided. At the end of every day, before I go to bed, I'm going to reflect back upon my day and I'm going to choose the most story worthy moment from that day and I'm going to write it down. Truthfully, the actual prompt I use in my mind is this. Someone has kidnapped my family, my wife and children. And they refuse to give them back until I take a stage and tell a story about something that happened over the course of the day, even if nothing story worthy has happened over the course of the day. I must find the most story worthy moment of all the unstory worthy moments of the day. And that is the one that I will record. Now, I don't want you to write the whole story out. I don't do that either. That's insanity. Those are

## [03:00 - 06:00]

the kinds of people who journal, right? They tend to journal a great deal right after a breakup, right? They're at home and they're heartbroken and they fill pages of their journal. And then suddenly they find love again and the journal sort of goes back into the drawer for the next heartbreak. I don't want you to be that kind of a person. I want homework for life to be equivalent to brushing your teeth. I want it to be short, easy and you would never skip it. If you're not skipping the brushing of your teeth, which you should not do, you're not going to skip homework for life either. So the way I do it is this, take a look at this worksheet. This is a sample of a worksheet from way back in 2015. You can see it's two columns. This is not complicated. There's a date column. I record the date and then my B column, I stretch across the screen and I only allow myself that sort of length of a computer screen to fill in my story. Just enough of the details so that I can remember what the moment was and then I move on with my day. My goal was one new story per month, 12 new stories over the course of the year. If that had happened, I would have been thrilled. Instead, something miraculous happens. I discover and thousands of people all over the world have discovered with me that our lives are filled with stories, moments where people say things to us that mean something. We look at the world in a new way we discover something about ourselves or our parents or our colleagues or our children that we never knew before. We suddenly change our mind. We used to think one thing and then suddenly we think another or maybe we saw something for the very first time, something in this world that we've never seen before. All of those moments are story worthy moments. My goal was to get 12 new stories over the course of a year. Instead, I find hundreds of stories over the course of a year. I've become a person who used to be worried that he'd run out of stories and now I'm a person who recognizes I will run out of time before I get to tell all my stories. It's the problem I want you to have as well. What happens is over time, you like I will develop a lens for storytelling and I for the world that you don't have right now, a recognition that meaningful things happen to us every day. The tragedy of our lives is this. We walk through our lives and oftentimes we don't even see the things that touch our hearts and touch our minds. Sometimes we get lucky enough to see them and when we do, maybe we tell a friend or two and then we forget them. And what happens is we end up being 60, 70, 80, 90 years old, we look back on our lives and we wonder where the time went. We can't believe how quickly time flies by.

## [06:01 - 09:01]

It doesn't. The problem is we don't mark time. We lose our days. We treat our days like they're worthless. We'll walk down the street, pick a quarter up off the ground and stuff it in our pocket. But someone will say something to us that really means something to us and will let it go and completely forget it. Play this terrible game with me. Take your age. I'm 50. Today we'll subtract 12. Take your age and subtract 12. So for me, I am thinking about my 38th year of my life right now. Whatever year you're thinking about right now, think about that year and think about how many memories you have from that year of your life. Do you have any? Do you have a handful? You probably have some. You went around the sun 365 days. I bet you're hard pressed to come up with a dozen memories from that year of your life. Unless you had a baby, changed a job, moved across the country, got married, got divorced. Unless you had one of those large life moments, you might have nothing from that year. Does that mean that the year was wasted? Does that mean you lived a bad year of your life? No, it doesn't. It means you didn't take note of the days. It means you walked through your life with blinders on, failing to pick up the things that deserved to be picked up. Homework for life says, take account for your day. Notice what's important. Find something that makes this day different than every other day. What you'll discover over time, like me, is our days are filled with these moments. I used to write down one moment per day. My average now is five. Every day I find five things worth remembering over the course of my day. Now, not everyone will be a story. About 8% of the items that I enter in my homework for life could legitimately be stories that I tell on a stage at a dinner party as part of a presentation. 8%. The others, though, are just as valuable. Sometimes they're anecdotes that I can use in side stories. Sometimes they're jokes that can make people laugh. Sometimes they're moments I just want to remember and hold on to forever, even if I never tell a soul about that moment. There's other advantages to homework for life, too. One of them is you'll begin to see patterns in your life and discover that you are tracked or occupying the space of a story that you don't even realize exists in your life. You'll discover that I do something all the time, but didn't realize

## [09:01 - 12:05]

I do it all the time. And now I have a story to tell, a good example. I tell people all the time that my wife Alicia and I never fight. And it's kind of disgustingly true. We've never raised our voices to each other. We very rarely disagree. She also always asks on the hottest day of the year. And so when she asks me, can you bring up the air conditioners? I say, no, it's like the hottest day of the year. I'm not going to do it today. I'll do it on another day. And she always says, okay, that's fine. And then I sit there and I stew. And then I go to the basement and I bring up the air conditioners, making as much noise as possible while doing so. And she'll say like, what's wrong? What's going on? I'm like, I'm bringing up the air conditioners. And she goes, oh, thank you. She has no idea that any of this is happening. So I enter that in my homework for life. A month later, Alicia asks me to mow the lawn because we're going to have a party for Charlie. It's the hottest day of the year. And she's asked me to mow the lawn. I just fight in a very unique way. I do it by engaging in chores that I don't want to do in a very loud way. And she has no idea that it's happening. That becomes a story. Another thing that homework for life will do for you is you'll start to see stories in your life that you can't see because they take place over the course of time.
